EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front resided across the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon and evening of June 4th, allowing moisture and instability to pool along the boundary. This combined with several strong upper level disturbances resulted in numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Many of these thunderstorms became severe. While penny sized was reported in spots, damaging winds from the thunderstorms was widespread. EF-1 tornadoes were confirmed near Stevensville in Culpeper County, in Millwood in Clarke County and near Hartwood in Stafford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey found a path of tree damage along Stony Hill Road and Route 522. Dozens of large trees were snapped and uprooted in the same direction. A utility company transmission tower was knocked down east of Route 522.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon of June 16. With a warm moist atmosphere in place, numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and evening. Damaging winds that lead to the downing of trees and power lines were common across north central Virginia. Some locations also received severe hail. Near Ruckersville, Virginia, baseball size hail lead to six inches of accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotters reported hail to the size of quarters in Belmont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon of June 16. With a warm moist atmosphere in place, numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and evening. Damaging winds that lead to the downing of trees and power lines were common across north central Virginia. Some locations also received severe hail. Near Ruckersville, Virginia, baseball size hail lead to six inches of accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported power lines down north of Bluemont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving into the area triggered a few thunderstorms, some of which became severe. These isolated severe thunderstorms brought wind damage to portions of southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed power lines in Seven Mile Ford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere set the stage for afternoon development of showers and thunderstorms on June 26. Some of these storms reached severe levels and generated large hail and damaging downburst winds.

The combination of a frontal boundary laying across the area and low pressure moving along that boundary and through the region, produced rainfall amounts between two and five inches over much of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts between one and four inches occurred across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a frontal boundary laying across the area and low pressure moving along that boundary and through the region, produced rainfall amounts between two and five inches over much of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts between one and four inches occurred across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system across the Ohio Valley during the afternoon of Thursday, May 8th, pushed east toward the mountains Thursday evening. A boundary was trailing from the low across southern Virginia. This put the region in a high shear environment, meaning, winds at the surface were blowing from the southeast, while a few thousand feet above the surface, the winds were blowing out of the southwest at 40 to 50 mph. Supercells formed in the foothills and piedmont of Virginia, ahead of a strong but broken line of thunderstorms that formed just west of the mountains and became more organized by the time it reached the Blue Ridge. These severe thunderstorms brought EF-1 tornadoes, damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding to portions of Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado, rated EF-1, with winds 86 to 95 mph, moved out of Henry County at 801 pm EDT and continued another half mile into Franklin County, before lifting at 803 pm EDT. The maximum width was 75 yards. Numerous trees were downed, with 2 homes damaged. The tornado lifted at state routes 618 and 632.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms, some severe, brought torrential rains to portions of Southwest Virginia, during the evening of May 8th, into the morning of May 9th. Up to 5 inches of rain fell in a few locations, leading to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flyblow Creek flooded a portion of Route 618/Farmers Road, one mile west of Renan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An uncharacteristic area of relatively cool low pressure moved southeast out of Canada into the region. Daytime heating due to sunshine combined with the relatively cooler low overhead and helped to result in a very unstable atmosphere. The result was the formation of thunderstorms with numerous reports of severe hail and damaging winds.

Thunderstorms developed the afternoon of June 14 in advance of an approaching cold front.  Some of these storms reached severe levels and produced large hail and also damaging winds that downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down two threes. The trees were six inches in diameter.  Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed on Highway 58 from Route 10/32 Exit to Route 642 Exit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced large hail and some damaging winds across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large outflow boundary from an overnight thunderstorm complex moved through the region during the morning and early afternoon hours of July 22. This prevented thunderstorm development until early evening when storms started developing. Meanwhile, a line of thunderstorms approached the area from the west. Outflow from the first storms generated scattered clusters of storms across the northwest NC and southwest VA Piedmont up to the Blue Ridge, while the more organized line of storms moved into the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Route 713.  Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon of June 22. Afternoon heating contributed to strong instability that helped to focus scattered strong to severe thunderstorms ahead of a cold front. Damaging winds brought some trees and power lines down in north central Virginia. Large hail was also reported. Hail to the size of quarters fell Opal and Stanley. The hail damaged 2 mobile homes near Dale City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A county official reported penny-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into the Mid Atlantic region on June 14. With strong afternoon heating, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of the front in the afternoon and evening. Some of the storms resulted in downed trees and power lines. In Leesburg, Virginia, nickel sized hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local newspaper reported that a lightning-sparked fire caused minor property damage east of Winchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moving northeast along the Blue Ridge the evening of the 11th triggered isolated thunderstorms across portions of southwest Virginia. Strong winds from one storm caused a tree to be blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell across Mill House Road blocking traffic for three hours and causing power outages to several area homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power pole was knocked down near the intersection of Pinetta and Enos Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hanna made landfall along the North Carolina/South Carolina border during the early morning of September 6th as a strong tropical storm. The system then proceeded to move rapidly north and then northeast along Interstate 95 reaching the southern Chesapeake Bay by the afternoon. The strongest winds with Hanna remained east of the center over eastern North Carolina and southeast Virginia while persistent bands of heavy rain developed east of the Blue Ridge. This axis of heavy rain persisted over much of the piedmont and foothills from the pre-dawn hours of Saturday morning through about midday. This resulted in impressive rainfall totals of between 3 and 6 inches in a narrow corridor over parts of the piedmont while a sharp gradient to an inch or less was seen closer to the eastern slopes of the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whitefall Road closed due to flooding of tributaries to the Bannister River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system crossed the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon and evening hours of May 8th. This system along with warm temperatures and high dew points triggered numerous strong to severe showers and thunderstorms across the region. Two tornadoes occurred in central Virginia. Several storms produced damaging winds that downed trees and power lines in portions of northern and central Virginia. A few locations reported flooding as storms trained over the same area through the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper reported several roads closed in Old Town Alexandria, including King Street and Prince Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced numerous tornadoes across central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down about a half mile east of Capron off Highway 58 near Douglas Drive. Several trees were downed or snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front combined with an upper level disturbance during the afternoon and evening hours of July 22nd. Cold air aloft combined with warm and humid air at the surface allowed for scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across the Mid Atlantic. Some storms became severe, downing trees and power lines and producing large hail across portions of the Shenandoah Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a carport on Elmwood Road near Winchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed and snapped in half near the intersection of Staples Mill Road and Bethlehem Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough for late July pushed a cold front through the region during the evening hours of the 23rd. Although instability was rather marginal...cooling aloft allowed for a broken line of severe convection to propagate across the Blue Ridge and into the Piedmont during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Morris Church Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed 2 miles west of Toano.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed along US 360 just west of Haynesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving into the southeast U.S. enhanced thunderstorms developing ahead of it. Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail over portions of southwest Virginia on June 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in Moss Run. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level area of low pressure moved across the region ahead of a weak upstream cold front. These features combined with an unstable air mass to produce widespread strong to severe thunderstorms that produced not only large hail but also included some wind damage on June 22. These severe storms lingered well after the loss of heating.

An unstable atmosphere set the stage for afternoon development of showers and thunderstorms on June 26. Some of these storms reached severe levels and generated large hail and damaging downburst winds.

An approaching cold front encountering an unstable air mass, triggered thunderstorms over southwest Virginia on June 10. Some of these storms produced damaging winds and hail up to the size of ping pongs.
Scattered severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds across portions of central and southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed on U.S. 1 between Mckenney and Dewitt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with an unstable atmosphere to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported just south of Broad Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed off Highway 40 between McKenney and Stony Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced penny to nickel size hail across portions of Southside Virginia.

A cold front approached the Mid Atlantic during the evening of June 3. Warm, moist and unstable air developed ahead of the front, allowing scattered strong to severe thunderstorms to develop. Several reports of downed trees and power lines were received. A roof was also blown off a house in Warrenton. One thunderstorm produced heavy rainfall that resulted in flash flooding in Mitchells in Culpeper County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Orange County Emergency Management reported trees down across roadways and power outages in extreme south central parts of the county near Gordonsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front resided across the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon and evening of June 4th, allowing moisture and instability to pool along the boundary. This combined with several strong upper level disturbances resulted in numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Many of these thunderstorms became severe. While penny sized was reported in spots, damaging winds from the thunderstorms was widespread. EF-1 tornadoes were confirmed near Stevensville in Culpeper County, in Millwood in Clarke County and near Hartwood in Stafford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed on Knole Court in Alexandria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon and evening of July 4th. This system combined with a weak surface front and heat and humidity to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms during the late afternoon hours. Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail in portions of north central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny-size hail was reported near Heflin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was entrenched across the Mid Atlantic during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 7th. As an upper level disturbance moved across the area, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed. Damaging winds brought down some trees and power lines across north central Virginia. The largest diameter hail recorded was quarter-sized, and occurred in Purcellville in Loudoun County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported quarter-sized hail at Routes 7 and 287 in Purcellville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front resided across the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon and evening of June 4th, allowing moisture and instability to pool along the boundary. This combined with several strong upper level disturbances resulted in numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Many of these thunderstorms became severe. While penny sized was reported in spots, damaging winds from the thunderstorms was widespread. EF-1 tornadoes were confirmed near Stevensville in Culpeper County, in Millwood in Clarke County and near Hartwood in Stafford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated observation at Fair Oaks Hospital measured a wind gust of 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front resided across the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon and evening of June 4th, allowing moisture and instability to pool along the boundary. This combined with several strong upper level disturbances resulted in numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Many of these thunderstorms became severe. While penny sized was reported in spots, damaging winds from the thunderstorms was widespread. EF-1 tornadoes were confirmed near Stevensville in Culpeper County, in Millwood in Clarke County and near Hartwood in Stafford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local newspaper reported roads in Brentsville closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level cold low had been slowly progressing into the region since July 7. On July 9, the center of the low was shifting east of the area and the feature's associated cold front was moving across the area. A combination good instability due to the low, plenty of moisture near the surface, relatively dry air aloft, and the passage of the cold front, resulted in storms that produced severe weather, especially in the form of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down in Randolph.  Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the region on July 27th as a strong upper level low pressure system remained over the northeast. Warm and humid air at the surface interacted with cooler air aloft to produce scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. Many of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds that downed trees and power lines. Large hail to the size of quarters was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotsylvania Fire and Rescue reported hail to the size of pennies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving into the central and southern Appalachians provided the focus for severe thunderstorm development during the morning and afternoon of August 2nd. Damaging winds, and hail up to the size of quarters occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Overlook Drive.
